quotes.text: The first time I heard my voice back on tape I genuinely thought the machine was broken. I called the engineer over twice. He was very patient with me about it.
quotes.speaker: Adele
quotes.context: The Jonathan Ross Show, ITV, Series 20, broadcast November 2021
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Adele's self-deprecating, conversational humor about her own career and voice is one of her most recognizable public traits, and this fits her chat-show register precisely. The sneaky part is that she's told a version of a 'first time hearing my voice back' story before, just not quite like this — the detail about calling the engineer over is what I invented.

quotes.text: Jazz musicians have this thing where we don't really clap for each other after solos — we nod. A nod from the right person in the right room means more than a standing ovation from a crowd that doesn't know what just happened.
quotes.speaker: Wynton Marsalis
quotes.context: Masterclass Q&A session at Juilliard School, New York, transcribed in DownBeat magazine, March 2018
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Wynton has spoken extensively about jazz culture, musician etiquette, and insider standards of excellence. The giveaway is the slight edge of elitism in the last clause, which is a touch sharper than Wynton's usual pedagogical warmth — he tends to be more generous about general audiences in documented quotes.

quotes.text: I don't hear samples the way other producers hear them. I hear the hole that's left in the original record — the space the producer didn't use. That's what I'm going after. What got left on the floor.
quotes.speaker: J Dilla
quotes.context: Interview with Brian Coleman published in Wax Poetics, Issue 14, 2005
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Dilla's documented philosophy around sampling and negative space in music makes this entirely consistent with how producers who knew him described his process. The giveaway for true heads: Dilla rarely gave long press interviews in this format; Wax Poetics coverage of him was mostly posthumous.

quotes.text: It's a lot like being a chef, in a way. You're trying to create food that satisfies your taste, but with a good awareness that there are people whose tastes are for more salt, or more spice — or for less.
quotes.speaker: Susan Rogers
quotes.context: Prince's former recording engineer describing the producer's balancing act, in a Grammy.com interview about her career and her book.

source.title: Grammy.com — Producer & Engineer Susan Rogers Worked With Prince And Barenaked Ladies
